deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 25-year-old man who presented with new-onset seizures.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient had three episodes of seizures.
The first two were just twitching of the head and neck, but the third one was more severe.
He had stiffness and lost consciousness, fell, and had full-body shaking.
He also had some facial fractures from the fall.
After that, he had another episode at another hospital with similar symptoms, and they gave him levetiracetam, which is an antiepileptic drug.
Looking at the imaging, the CT showed nasal bone fractures, but no intracranial issues.
The MRI showed restricted diffusion in the right superior frontal gyrus, which suggests possible infarction or edema.
The T2 images showed hyperintensity in the right frontal lobe, consistent with vasogenic edema.
The venogram showed thrombosis or slow flow in the superior sagittal sinus, and the susceptibility-weighted images showed cortical vein thrombosis.
He has a history of a car accident three years ago, which could have caused a concussion.
He was evaluated for Marfan's syndrome but tests were normal.
He also had ectopia lentis, which is associated with Marfan's, but again, his heart was normal.
No family history of clotting disorders, so that's less likely.
The physical exam shows some facial features that might be consistent with Marfan's, like a high-arched palate and pectus excavatum.
His arm span is long, which is another feature.
His blood work shows normal clotting studies, so no bleeding tendency.
Putting it all together, the MRI and venogram point towards a venous sinus thrombosis, likely in the sagittal sinus.
This can cause seizures, especially if there's hypoperfusion.
The fact that he had two episodes of seizures and the imaging showing venous issues makes me think of sinus venous thrombosis, which can lead to stroke-like presentations or seizures.
The facial features and history of possible Marfan's might be a red flag for underlying connective tissue issues, but the immediate issue seems to be the venous thrombosis.

deepseek-r1-distill-qwen-14b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 25-year-old man who presented with new-onset seizures.
Let me go through the case step by step.
First, the patient had episodes of twitching, starting with the head and neck, then progressing to full-body shaking and loss of consciousness.
He had a few episodes, each getting more severe.
The imaging studies are key here.
The CT showed nasal bone fractures but no intracranial issues or cervical spine injuries.

The MRI was more concerning: restricted diffusion in the right superior frontal gyrus, vasogenic edema in the adjacent frontal lobe, and signs of cortical vein thrombosis on the susceptibility-weighted images.
The MR venography showed slow flow or thrombosis in the superior sagittal sinus.
So, the imaging points towards some kind of cerebrovascular issue.
The restricted diffusion and vasogenic edema suggest an infarction or some kind of ischemic event.
The cortical vein thrombosis and the superior sagittal sinus thrombosis point towards venous sinus thrombosis, which can lead to venous infarction.
Looking at the history, he had a previous ophthalmologic issue with ectopia lentis, which is associated with Marfan syndrome.
Marfan is a connective tissue disorder that can affect the aorta and other blood vessels, making them prone to aneurysms and dissections.
It's also associated with dural ectasia, which can compress venous sinuses and lead to thrombosis.
His family history includes a grandfather who died of a stroke, which might hint at a predisposition to vascular issues.
He's tall, with long arms and a high arched palate, which are features of Marfan.
The striae on his back and pectus excavatum also support this.
The lab results show a high d-dimer, which is indicative of recent clotting.
His prothrombin time and INR are elevated, suggesting possible clotting issues or hypercoagulability.

Putting it all together, the patient's symptoms, imaging findings, and history, especially the features of Marfan syndrome, point towards cerebral venous thrombosis, specifically involving the superior sagittal sinus and cortical veins.
This is likely due to the underlying connective tissue disorder predisposing him to venous sinus thrombosis.
